messages.content: I want the pricing page to sound less like we are selling models and more like we are selling compounding memory.

attachments.label: Pricing Page Feedback
attachments.text:
Prospects responded best when pricing language connected directly to repeated value rather than model access alone.

Words like durable, local, and compounding resonated more than premium.

messages.content: Lead with durable value: every useful chat should leave behind notes, links, and a graph that feels more helpful over time. That lines up with [[Pricing Notes]] and [[Trust Signals]].
Can you tighten that into three page sections and keep the tone calm?
Yes. I would structure it as promise, proof, and boundaries: what compounds, how it stays local-first, and what remains visible when cloud features are unavailable.
I need a two-day retreat agenda for product decisions that have been dragging.
Anchor the retreat around [[Founder OS]], [[Product Council]], and [[Weekly Review Rhythm]]. Day one should narrow the next quarter's bets. Day two should test which rituals keep those bets alive.
I also want a short prep memo for everyone.
Frame the memo around open tensions, not polished answers. Ask each person to bring one decision, one risk, and one piece of design partner evidence.
Support tickets keep piling up without enough structure. I need a calmer tagging scheme.
Group tags around product signals instead of surfaces: onboarding, trust, and compounding value. That mirrors [[Support Inbox Patterns]] and [[Synthesis Support Signals]].
Can you suggest a weekly review loop for that?
Review the tags once a week, summarize the top three shifts, and route only those themes into roadmap discussion.
I need a better follow-up note after today's design partner calls.

attachments.label: Ada Partner Call
attachments.text:
Ada wanted the app to explain what changed after a chat without forcing her to inspect each note manually.

She also preferred warmer, steadier interface language.

attachments.label: Maple Clinic Call
attachments.text:
Maple Clinic cared about local storage boundaries first, then workflow speed.

The team said trust would depend on whether the product could fail gracefully when cloud services disappeared.

messages.content: Summarize each call into one sharp need, one trust concern, and one workflow moment. That keeps [[Field Notes Design Partners]] compact and comparable.
Ada and Maple both cared about boundaries, but in different language.
Name the shared theme as trust and keep the phrasing separate underneath so you do not flatten the nuance too early.
New users still stare at an empty app. I need a first-run checklist that does not feel technical.
Keep it to three visible steps: choose a vault, start a first chat, and open the first generated note. That directly addresses [[Onboarding Friction]].
What language would you use?
Use steady verbs: choose, ask, open. Avoid setup-heavy language unless a failure state requires more detail.
I keep saying notes from chats compound, but the product still explains it awkwardly.
Show the sequence plainly: chat, extract, link, revisit. [[Notes From Chats]] should feel like a working loop, not a hidden feature.
What should the graph say in that frame?
That it is a reading surface for the growing body of notes, not just a map for its own sake.
I need a short local-first pitch that feels practical, not ideological.
Describe what the user keeps: local chats, local notes, and usefulness during outages. That is the strongest expression of [[Local First Principles]].
What should I avoid saying?
Avoid implying the cloud disappears. The strength is that the local layer remains trustworthy even when the cloud is unavailable.
How should we explain usage limits without making the product feel stingy?
Tie limits to predictable operating costs and keep the upgrade language anchored in value moments, not fear. [[Pricing Notes]] and [[Trust Signals]] both point that way.
Can I say premium models in the first sentence?
Only after you've reminded the reader what the product helps them keep.
I want a tighter weekly review template for the team.
Use four blocks: what compounded, where users hesitated, what changed in the graph, and what gets dropped next week. That links [[Weekly Review Rhythm]] to [[Retention Loop]].
Should support sit inside that or separate?
Keep support inside as evidence, not as its own silo.
I'm hiring a product engineer and the brief still sounds generic.

Name the actual edge: someone who can tighten product language, reason about local-first architecture, and turn user signals into implementation decisions. [[Hiring Product Engineer]] needs that specificity.
Should I mention design taste directly?
Yes, but tie it to concrete responsibilities like onboarding, failure states, and note workflows.
Give me a short retro outline after launch week.

attachments.label: Launch Week Retro
attachments.text:
Support volume was low, but confusion clustered around what counted as saved knowledge versus transient chat.

The next launch should explain the compounding loop earlier.

messages.content: Start with what users understood immediately, where support questions clustered, and what the team had to explain repeatedly. [[Launch Week Checklist]] and [[Support Inbox Patterns]] are the right anchors.
Can we turn that into next sprint input?
Yes. Translate each repeated explanation into one product or copy decision.
I want meeting notes to become more reusable. Right now they just pile up.
Shorten the residue. Pull out decisions, tensions, and follow-up notes instead of preserving full transcripts. [[Meeting Distillation]] and [[Ambient Capture]] should drive the pattern.
What if I still want access to the raw material?
Keep raw material in the vault, but make the working note the obvious entry point.
Help me audit the product for trust signals before the next release.
Review every place the app touches uncertainty: loading, empty states, cloud failures, and where notes are written. [[Trust Signals]] should connect those moments into one standard.
What's the quickest pass?
Look for places where the app implies progress without showing what changed.
People either love the graph or ignore it. I need a clearer framing.
Position it as a reading ritual for connected ideas, not a universal control panel. That is the core of [[Graph Reading Ritual]].
What should the empty state say?
Invite the user to start with one note cluster or recent session, not the whole graph at once.
River Studio liked the product but worried about where context lives between projects.
That suggests we should sharpen the vault story and explain project separation more clearly. [[River Studio]] connects nicely to [[Vault Layout]] and [[Trust Signals]].
Anything else worth logging?
Yes: they treated repeatable recovery of context as the real wow moment, not the model output itself.
They also asked whether each client could have its own vault without losing the cross-project references the team relies on.

Capture that as a structural tension: separation by default, intentional bridges when the work genuinely overlaps. [[Vault Layout]] should explain the boundary, while [[River Studio]] can hold the agency-specific examples.
What's the cleanest follow-up question for the next call?

Ask them to walk through one real handoff: where context currently lives, when it gets lost, and what they would need to trust a vault-per-client setup. That will surface whether the problem is navigation, permissions, or retrieval.
Maple Clinic reacted strongly to the local-first story. I want a succinct readout.

attachments.label: Maple Clinic Call
attachments.text:
Maple Clinic cared about local storage boundaries first, then workflow speed.

The team said trust would depend on whether the product could fail gracefully when cloud services disappeared.

messages.content: Record the priority order clearly: local storage boundaries first, offline usefulness second, workflow speed third. [[Maple Clinic]] and [[Source Summary Privacy Report]] should carry that thread.
Should the product copy mention clinics specifically?
Not yet. Keep the copy general and let the case notes remain specific.
They kept asking what still works if cloud models are down for the day.

That is worth highlighting because it turns local-first from philosophy into an operational promise. Tie the readout to note access, search, and the ability to continue organizing the vault even when generation pauses.
Should I capture the compliance angle too or is that too early?
Capture it as an emerging theme, not a product claim. Say that explicit storage boundaries lowered anxiety, while formal compliance questions still need separate validation.
I want office hours to produce more than scattered follow-ups.
Ask one product question each week, log patterns in the same format, and connect the output back into [[Office Hours Format]] and [[Support Inbox Patterns]].
What's the smallest template?
Theme, quote, friction, next decision.
I also want the notes to roll up into something the team can scan in five minutes.

Then standardize the residue. End each session with one sentence on the user's goal, one quote, one friction point, and the next decision it informs. That gives [[Office Hours Format]] a repeatable shape.
Could that feed the weekly review automatically?
Yes, if the format stays consistent. Treat office hours as a lightweight evidence stream that lands inside [[Weekly Review Rhythm]] instead of becoming a separate reporting ritual.
The roadmap is sprawling again. Help me trim it to three bets.

Bias toward compounding loops: stronger first-run clarity, better note extraction, and clearer trust boundaries. That keeps [[Trellis Roadmap]] aligned with [[Activation Metrics]] and [[Trust Signals]].
What do I defer?
Anything that adds surface area without making the core loop easier to understand or revisit.
I keep wanting to add collaboration polish and more graph controls because they feel strategic.

They may be strategic later, but right now they widen the product before the core loop is dependable. Ask whether each idea helps a new user move from first chat to a revisitable note with more confidence.
Can you restate the three bets as outcomes instead of features?

Yes: new users understand what to do within minutes, important conversations turn into useful notes without cleanup, and people trust where their context lives when cloud features or providers are unavailable.
